islands.name: Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
islands.blurb: The Seven Wonders of the Ancient World is a list of seven notable structures present during classical antiquity, first established in the 1572 publication Octo Mundi Miracula: the Great Pyramid of Giza, the Colossus of Rhodes, the Lighthouse of Alexandria, the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus, the Temple of Artemis, the Statue of Zeus at Olympia and the Hanging Gardens of Babylon. Of the seven, only the Pyramid of Giza, by far the oldest, remains standing; the others have been destroyed over the centuries, and every gallery here is instead hung with the art through which later ages imagined them.

artists.name: The Great Pyramid of Giza
artists.bio: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the largest of the Egyptian pyramids, built c. 2600 BC over a period of about 26 years as the tomb of the Fourth Dynasty pharaoh Khufu. Initially standing 146.6 metres tall, it was the world's tallest human-made structure for more than 3,700 years. It is the oldest of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World and the only wonder that has remained largely intact.
artists.whyTheyMatter: The oldest of the Seven Wonders — and the only one still standing.

artists.name: The Hanging Gardens of Babylon
artists.bio: The Hanging Gardens were described as a remarkable feat of engineering — an ascending series of tiered gardens of trees, shrubs and vines, resembling a large green mountain of mud bricks. Legend holds they were built in Babylon by the Neo-Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 605–562 BC) for his Median wife Amytis, who missed the green hills of her homeland. They are the only wonder whose location has never been definitively established: no extant Babylonian texts mention them, and they may have been purely mythical, destroyed around the first century AD, or in fact a garden built by King Sennacherib at Nineveh.
artists.whyTheyMatter: The one wonder that may never have existed — a legend of engineered paradise painted and repainted for five centuries.

artists.name: The Temple of Artemis
artists.bio: The Temple of Artemis, or Temple of Diana, was a Greek temple at Ephesus dedicated to a localised form of the goddess Artemis. An elaborate reconstruction begun around 550 BCE under the Cretan architect Chersiphron, funded by Croesus of Lydia, was burned down in 356 BCE by an arsonist popularly identified as Herostratus; the Ephesians rebuilt it larger still, with more than 127 columns. The last incarnation of the temple was destroyed in 401 CE, and only foundations and fragments remain today.
artists.whyTheyMatter: Central to Ephesian life and rebuilt again and again — for ancient list-makers the most marvellous temple in the world.

artists.name: The Statue of Zeus at Olympia
artists.bio: The Statue of Zeus at Olympia was a giant seated figure about 12.4 metres tall, made by the Greek sculptor Phidias around 435 BC and erected in the Temple of Zeus at Olympia. It was a chryselephantine sculpture of ivory plates and gold panels over a wooden framework, with Zeus enthroned on painted cedarwood ornamented with ebony, ivory, gold and precious stones. The statue was lost and destroyed before the end of the 6th century AD — one Byzantine tradition says it was carried to Constantinople and burned in the great fire of the Palace of Lausus in 475 AD — and its form is known only from ancient descriptions and coins.
artists.whyTheyMatter: Phidias' enthroned god was so celebrated that no copy survives — every image of it is an act of imagination guided by coins.

artists.name: The Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
artists.bio: The Mausoleum at Halicarnassus was a tomb built between 353 and 351 BC in present-day Bodrum, Turkey, for Mausolus, a satrap in the Achaemenid Persian Empire, and his sister-wife Artemisia II. Designed by the Greek architects Satyros of Paros and Pythius of Priene, it stood about 45 metres high, carried sculptural reliefs by four famous Greek sculptors and contained 400 freestanding sculptures. Destroyed by successive earthquakes between the 12th and 15th centuries, it was the last surviving of the six destroyed wonders, and its owner's name became the generic word for an above-ground tomb.
artists.whyTheyMatter: A tomb so magnificent that its owner's name became the word for every grand tomb after it.

artists.name: The Colossus of Rhodes
artists.bio: The Colossus of Rhodes was a statue of the sun god Helios, erected in the city of Rhodes by Chares of Lindos in 280 BC to celebrate the island's successful defence against the year-long siege of Demetrius I of Macedon. Standing approximately 33 metres high — about two-thirds of the Statue of Liberty from feet to crown — it was the tallest statue in the ancient world. It collapsed at the knees in the earthquake of 226 BC and, in accordance with the Oracle of Delphi, was never rebuilt; the fallen remains lay in place for over eight centuries.
artists.whyTheyMatter: The tallest statue of antiquity, standing for just 54 years yet inspiring images — and the Statue of Liberty — ever since.

artists.name: The Lighthouse of Alexandria
artists.bio: The Lighthouse of Alexandria, or Pharos, was built by the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt during the reign of Ptolemy II Philadelphus (280–247 BC) and has been estimated at no less than 100 metres in overall height, for many centuries one of the world's tallest man-made structures. Severely damaged by three earthquakes between AD 956 and 1303, it became an abandoned ruin and survived in part until 1480, when the last of its stones were used to build the Citadel of Qaitbay on the same site. French archaeologists discovered remains of the lighthouse on the sea floor of Alexandria's Eastern Harbour in 1994.
artists.whyTheyMatter: The archetype of every lighthouse since — the island's name, Pharos, still means 'lighthouse' in many languages.
